events.title: New York City is the cultural capital of the world and art shouldn’t be a luxury
events.description: New York City is the cultural capital of the world and art shouldn’t be a luxury only some of us can afford. I’m grateful for Under the Radar Theater Festival’s generosity and partnership towards that vision as they announce 1,500 free tickets for New Yorkers. Check out https://utrfest.org/under-the-radar-for-all/ for details on how to claim one today!

events.title: Spiritually Attacked: Becoming the Calm in the Storm (online)
events.description:
There are moments in life when it feels like everything is coming at you at once.

Heavy emotions. Strange tension. Sudden conflict. Mental noise. Exhaustion. Fear that does not seem to belong to the moment. Sometimes people call it stress. Sometimes they call it burnout. And sometimes, deeper down, it feels spiritual.

This session is for those moments.

In this Shurch gathering, we will explore what people mean when they say they feel “spiritually attacked” and how to respond without collapsin

**Title:** Implementing Financial Algorithms on IonQ devices
This presentation will highlight IonQ's latest progress in leveraging novel quantum computing algorithms for financial industry challenges, such as the standard problem of discrete portfolio optimization. events.title: Word and Object by Quine Week 1
events.description: How does language come to have meaning and can we trust natural language to be a reliable vehicle for truth? For many in the early analytic tradition, the answer to the meaning of language is intimately related to a correspondence theory of truth where one needs to painstakingly tie words to their reference objects in the world. For these philosophers, logic is the correct tool in order to tame natural language and train it to be a reliable vehicle for truth. For Quine (1908-2000), this is simpl

events.title: To Have or To Be? by Erich Fromm (week 2)
events.description: *"To Have or To Be?"* is one of the seminal books of the second half of the 20th century. His thesis is that two modes of existence struggle for the spirit of humankind: the having mode, which concentrates on material possessions, power, and aggression, and is the basis of the universal evils of greed, envy, and violence; and the being mode, which is based on love, the pleasure of sharing, and in productive activity. "*To Have or To Be?"* is nothing less than a manifesto for a new social and psy
